title: Balder Dead (An Episode)
content:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ SENDING

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So on the floor lay Balder dead; and round
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Lay thickly strewn swords axes darts and spears
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Which all the Gods in sport had idly thrown
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ At Balder, whom no weapon piercן¿½d or clove:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But in his breast stood fixt the fatal bough
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of mistletoe, which Lok the Accuser gave
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To Hoder, and unwitting Hoder threw:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Gainst that alone had Balderן¿½s life no charm.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And all the Gods and all the Heroes came
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And stood round Balder on the bloody floor
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Weeping and wailing; and Valhalla rang
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Up to its golden roof with sobs and cries:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And on the tables stood the untasted meats,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And in the horns and gold-rimmן¿½d skulls the wine:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And now would Night have fallן¿½n, and found them yet
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Wailing; but otherwise was Odinן¿½s will:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And thus the Father of the Ages spake:

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Enough of tears, ye Gods, enough of wail!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Not to lament in was Valhalla made.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ If any here might weep for Balderן¿½s death
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I most might weep, his Father; such a son
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I lose today, so bright, so lovן¿½d a God.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But he has met that doom which long ago
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The Nornies, when his mother bare him, spun,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And Fate set seal, that so his end must be.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Balder has met his death, and ye survive:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Weep him an hour; but what can grief avail?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For you yourselves, ye Gods, shall meet your doom,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ All ye who hear me, and inhabit Heaven,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And I too, Odin too, the Lord of all;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But ours we shall not meet, when that day comes,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With womanן¿½s tears and weak complaining cries
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Why should we meet anotherן¿½s portion so?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Rather it fits you, having wept your hour,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With cold dry eyes, and hearts composן¿½d and stern,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To live, as erst, your daily life in Heaven:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ By me shall vengeance on the murderer Lok,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The Foe, the Accuser, whom, though Gods, we hate,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Be strictly carן¿½d for, in the appointed day.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Meanwhile, to-morrow, when the morning dawns,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Bring wood to the seashore to Balderן¿½s ship,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And on the deck build high a funeral pile,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And on the top lay Balderן¿½s corpse, and put
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Fire to the wood, and send him out to sea
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To burn; for that is what the dead desire.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So having spoke, the King of Gods arose
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And mounted his horse Sleipner, whom he rode,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And from the hall of Heaven he rode away
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To Lidskialf, and sate upon his throne,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The Mount, from whence his eye surveys the world.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And far from Heaven he turnן¿½d his shining orbs
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To look on Midgard, and the earth, and men:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And on the conjuring Lapps he bent his gaze
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Whom antlerן¿½d reindeer pull over the snow;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And on the Finns, the gentlest of mankind,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Fair men, who live in holes under the ground:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Nor did he look once more to Idaן¿½s plain,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Nor towards Valhalla, and the sorrowing Gods;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For well he knew the Gods would heed his word,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And cease to mourn, and think of Balderן¿½s pyre.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But in Valhalla all the Gods went back
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ From around Balder, all the Heroes went;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And left his body stretchן¿½d upon the floor.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And on their golden chairs they sate again,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Beside the tables, in the hall of Heaven;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And before each the cooks who servן¿½d them placן¿½d
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ New messes of the boar Serimnerן¿½s flesh,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And the Valkyries crownן¿½d their horns with mead.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So they, with pent-up hearts and tearless eyes,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Wailing no more, in silence ate and drank,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ While Twilight fell, and sacred Night came on.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But the blind Hoder left the feasting Gods
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In Odinן¿½s hall, and went through Asgard streets,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And past the haven where the Gods have moorן¿½d
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Their ships, and through the gate, beyond the wall.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Though sightless, yet his own mind led the God.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Down to the margin of the roaring sea
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He came, and sadly went along the sand
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Between the waves and black oן¿½erhanging cliffs
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Where in and out the screaming seafowl fly;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Until he came to where a gully breaks
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Through the cliff wall, and a fresh stream runs down
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ From the high moors behind, and meets the sea.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ There in the glen Fensaler stands, the house
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of Frea, honourן¿½d Mother of the Gods,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And shows its lighted windows to the main.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ There he went up, and passן¿½d the open doors:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And in the hall he found those women old,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The Prophetesses, who by rite eterne
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ On Freaן¿½s hearth feed high the sacred fire
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Both night and day; and by the inner wall
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Upon her golden chair the Mother sate,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With folded hands, revolving things to come:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To her drew Hoder near, and spake, and said:

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Mother, a child of bale thou barן¿½st in me.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For, first, thou barest me with blinded eyes.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Sightless and helpless, wandering weak in Heaven;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And, after that, of ignorant witless mind
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Thou barest me, and unforeseeing soul:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That I alone must take the branch from Lok,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The Foe, the Accuser, whom, though Gods, we hate,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And cast it at the dear-lovן¿½d Balderן¿½s breast
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ At whom the Gods in sport their weapons threw
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Gainst that alone had Balderן¿½s life no charm.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Now therefore what to attempt, or whither fly?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For who will bear my hateful sight in Heaven?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Can I, O Mother, bring them Balder back
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Or for thou knowן¿½st the Fates, and things allowן¿½d
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Can I with Helaן¿½s power a compact strike,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And make exchange, and give my life for his?ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He spoke: the Mother of the Gods replied:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Hoder, ill-fated, child of bale, my son,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Sightless in soul and eye, what words are these?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That one, long portionן¿½d with his doom of death,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Should change his lot, and fill anotherן¿½s life,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And Hela yield to this, and let him go!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ On Balder Death hath laid her hand, not thee;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Nor doth she count this life a price for that.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For many Gods in Heaven, not thou alone,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Would freely die to purchase Balder back,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And wend themselves to Helaן¿½s gloomy realm.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For not so gladsome is that life in Heaven
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Which Gods and Heroes lead, in feast and fray,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Waiting the darkness of the final times,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That one should grudge its loss for Balderן¿½s sake,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Balder their joy, so bright, so lovן¿½d a God.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But Fate withstands, and laws forbid this way.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Yet in my secret mind one way I know,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Nor do I judge if it shall win or fail:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But much must still be tried, which shall but fail.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And the blind Hoder answerן¿½d her, and said:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½What way is this, O Mother, that thou showן¿½st?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Is it a matter which a God might try?ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And straight the Mother of the Gods replied:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½There is a way which leads to Helaן¿½s realm,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Untrodden, lonely, far from light and Heaven.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Who goes that way must take no other horse
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To ride, but Sleipner, Odinן¿½s horse, alone.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Nor must he choose that common path of Gods
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Which every day they come and go in Heaven,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Oן¿½er the bridge Bifrost, where is Heimdallן¿½s watch,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Past Midgard Fortress, down to Earth and men;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But he must tread a dark untravellן¿½d road
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Which branches from the north of Heaven, and ride
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Nine days, nine nights, towards the northern ice,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Through valleys deep-engulphן¿½d, with roaring streams.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And he will reach on the tenth morn a bridge
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Which spans with golden arches Giallן¿½s stream,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Not Bifrost, but that bridge a Damsel keeps,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Who tells the passing troops of dead their way
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To the low shore of ghosts, and Helaן¿½s realm.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And she will bid him northward steer his course:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Then he will journey through no lighted land,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Nor see the sun arise, nor see it set;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But he must ever watch the northern Bear
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Who from her frozen height with jealous eye
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Confronts the Dog and Hunter in the south,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And is alone not dipt in Oceanן¿½s stream.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And straight he will come down to Oceanן¿½s strand;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Ocean, whose watery ring enfolds the world,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And on whose marge the ancient Giants dwell.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But he will reach its unknown northern shore,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Far, far beyond the outmost Giantן¿½s home,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ At the chinkן¿½d fields of ice, the waste of snow:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And he will fare across the dismal ice
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Northward, until he meets a stretching wall
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Barring his way, and in the wall a grate.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But then he must dismount, and on the ice
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Tighten the girths of Sleipner, Odinן¿½s horse,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And make him leap the grate, and come within.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And he will see stretch round him Helaן¿½s realm,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The plains of Niflheim, where dwell the dead,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And hear the roaring of the streams of Hell.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And he will see the feeble shadowy tribes,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And Balder sitting crownן¿½d, and Helaן¿½s throne.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Then he must not regard the wailful ghosts
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Who all will flit, like eddying leaves, around;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But he must straight accost their solemn Queen,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And pay her homage, and entreat with prayers,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Telling her all that grief they have in Heaven
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For Balder, whom she holds by right below:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ If haply he may melt her heart with words,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And make her yield, and give him Balder back.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ She spoke: but Hoder answerן¿½d her and said:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Mother, a dreadful way is this thou showן¿½st.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ No journey for a sightless God to go.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And straight the Mother of the Gods replied:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Therefore thyself thou shalt not go, my son.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But he whom first thou meetest when thou comן¿½st
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To Asgard, and declarן¿½st this hidden way,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Shall go, and I will be his guide unseen.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ She spoke, and on her face let fall her veil,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And bowן¿½d her head, and sate with folded hands.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But at the central hearth those Women old,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Who while the Mother spake had ceased their toil,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Began again to heap the sacred fire:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And Hoder turnן¿½d, and left his motherן¿½s house,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Fensaler, whose lit windows look to sea;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And came again down to the roaring waves,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And back along the beach to Asgard went,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Pondering on that which Frea said should be.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But Night came down, and darkenן¿½d Asgard streets.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Then from their loathed feast the Gods arose,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And lighted torches, and took up the corpse
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of Balder from the floor of Odinן¿½s hall,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And laid it on a bier, and bare him home
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Through the fast-darkening streets to his own house
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Breidablik, on whose columns Balder gravן¿½d
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The enchantments, that recall the dead to life:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For wise he was, and many curious arts,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Postures of runes, and healing herbs he knew;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Unhappy: but that art he did not know
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To keep his own life safe, and see the sun:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ There to his hall the Gods brought Balder home,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And each bespake him as he laid him down:

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Would that ourselves, O Balder, we were borne
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Home to our halls, with torchlight, by our kin,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So thou mightן¿½st live, and still delight the Gods.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ They spake: and each went home to his own house.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But there was one, the first of all the Gods
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For speed, and Hermod was his name in Heaven;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Most fleet he was, but now he went the last,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Heavy in heart for Balder, to his house
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Which he in Asgard built him, there to dwell,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Against the harbour, by the city wall:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Him the blind Hoder met, as he came up
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ From the sea cityward, and knew his step;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Nor yet could Hermod see his brotherן¿½s face,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For it grew dark; but Hoder touchן¿½d his arm:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And as a spray of honeysuckle flowers
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Brushes across a tired travellerן¿½s face
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Who shuffles through the deep dew-moistenן¿½d dust,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ On a May evening, in the darkenן¿½d lanes,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And starts him, that he thinks a ghost went by
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So Hoder brushן¿½d by Hermodן¿½s side, and said:

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Take Sleipner, Hermod, and set forth with dawn
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To Helaן¿½s kingdom, to ask Balder back;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And they shall be thy guides, who have the power.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He spake, and brushן¿½d soft by, and disappearן¿½d.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And Hermod gazן¿½d into the night, and said:

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Who is it utters through the dark his hest
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So quickly, and will wait for no reply?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The voice was like the unhappy Hoderן¿½s voice.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Howbeit I will see, and do his hest;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For there rang note divine in that command.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So speaking, the fleet-footed Hermod came
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Home, and lay down to sleep in his own house,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And all the Gods lay down in their own homes.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And Hoder too came home, distraught with grief,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Loathing to meet, at dawn, the other Gods:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And he went in, and shut the door, and fixt
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ His sword upright, and fell on it, and died.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But from the hill of Lidskialf Odin rose,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The throne, from which his eye surveys the world;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And mounted Sleipner, and in darkness rode
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To Asgard. And the stars came out in Heaven,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ High over Asgard, to light home the King.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But fiercely Odin gallopן¿½d, movן¿½d in heart;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And swift to Asgard, to the gate, he came
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And terribly the hoofs of Sleipner rang
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Along the flinty floor of Asgard streets;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And the Gods trembled on their golden beds
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Hearing the wrathful Father coming home;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For dread, for like a whirlwind, Odin came:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And to Valhallaן¿½s gate he rode, and left
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Sleipner; and Sleipner went to his own stall:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And in Valhalla Odin laid him down.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But in Breidablik Nanna, Balderן¿½s wife,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Came with the Goddesses who wrought her will,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And stood round Balder lying on his bier:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And at his head and feet she stationן¿½d Scalds
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Who in their lives were famous for their song;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ These oן¿½er the corpse intonן¿½d a plaintive strain,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A dirge; and Nanna and her train replied.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And far into the night they wailן¿½d their dirge:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But when their souls were satisfied with wail,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ They went, and laid them down, and Nanna went
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Into an upper chamber, and lay down;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And Frea sealן¿½d her tired lids with sleep.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And ן¿½twas when Night is bordering hard on Dawn,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ When air is chilliest, and the stars sunk low,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Then Balderן¿½s spirit through the gloom drew near,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In garb, in form, in feature as he was
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Alive, and still the rays were round his head
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Which were his glorious mark in Heaven; he stood
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Over against the curtain of the bed,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And gazן¿½d on Nanna as she slept, and spake:

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Poor lamb, thou sleepest, and forgettן¿½st thy woe.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Tears stand upon the lashes of thine eyes,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Tears wet the pillow by thy cheek; but thou,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Like a young child, hast cried thyself to sleep.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Sleep on: I watch thee, and am here to aid.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Alive I kept not far from thee, dear soul,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Neither do I neglect thee now, though dead.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For with to-morrowן¿½s dawn the Gods prepare
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To gather wood, and build a funeral pile
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Upon my ship, and burn my corpse with fire,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That sad, sole honour of the dead; and thee
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ They think to burn, and all my choicest wealth,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With me, for thus ordains the common rite:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But it shall not be so: but mild, but swift,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But painless shall a stroke from Frea come,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To cut thy thread of life, and free thy soul,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And they shall burn thy corpse with mine, not thee.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And well I know that by no stroke of death,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Tardy or swift, wouldst thou be loath to die,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So it restorן¿½d thee, Nanna, to my side,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Whom thou so well hast lovן¿½d; but I can smooth
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Thy way, and this at least my prayers avail.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Yes, and I fain would altogether ward
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Death from thy head, and with the Gods in Heaven
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Prolong thy life, though not by thee desirן¿½d:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But Right bars this, not only thy desire.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Yet dreary, Nanna, is the life they lead
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In that dim world, in Helaן¿½s mouldering realm;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And doleful afire the ghosts, the troops of dead,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Whom Hela with austere control presides;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For of the race of Gods is no one there
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Save me alone, and Hela, solemn Queen:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And all the nobler souls of mortal men
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ On battle-field have met their death, and now
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Feast in Valhalla, in my Fatherן¿½s hall;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Only the inglorious sort are there below,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The old, the cowards, and the weak are there,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Men spent by sickness, or obscure decay.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But even there, O Nanna, we might find
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Some solace in each otherן¿½s look and speech,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Wandering together through that gloomy world.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And talking of the life we led in Heaven,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ While we yet livן¿½d, among the other Gods.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He spake, and straight his lineaments began
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To fade: and Nanna in her sleep stretchן¿½d out
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Her arms towards him with a cry; but he
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Mournfully shook his head, and disappearן¿½d.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And as the woodman sees a little smoke
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Hang in the air, afield, and disappear
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So Balder faded in the night away.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And Nanna on her bed sunk back: but then
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Frea, the Mother of the Gods, with stroke
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Painless and swift, set free her airy soul,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Which took, on Balderן¿½s track, the way below:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And instantly the sacred Morn appearן¿½d.



ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ II

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ JOURNEY TO THE DEAD

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ FORTH from the East, up the ascent of Heaven,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Day drove his courser with the Shining Mane;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And in Valhalla, from his gable perch,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The golden-crested Cock began to crow:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Hereafter, in the blackest dead of night,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With shrill and dismal cries that Bird shall crow,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Warning the Gods that foes draw nigh to Heaven;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But now he crew at dawn, a cheerful note,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To wake the Gods and Heroes to their tasks.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And all the Gods, and all the Heroes, woke.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And from their beds the Heroes rose, and donnן¿½d
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Their arms, and led their horses from the stall,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And mounted them, and in Valhallaן¿½s court
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Were rangן¿½d; and then the daily fray began.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And all day long they there are hackן¿½d and hewn
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Mid dust, and groans, and limbs loppן¿½d off, and blood;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But all at night return to Odinן¿½s hall
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Woundless and fresh: such lot is theirs in Heaven.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And the Valkyries on their steeds went forth
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Toward Earth and fights of men; and at their side
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Skulda, the youngest of the Nornies, rode:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And over Bifrost, where is Heimdallן¿½s watch,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Past Midgard Fortress, down to Earth they came:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ There through some battle-field, where men fall fast,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Their horses fetlock-deep in blood, they ride,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And pick the bravest warriors out for death,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Whom they bring back with them at night to Heaven,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To glad the Gods, and feast in Odinן¿½s hall.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But the Gods went not now, as otherwhile,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Into the Tilt-Yard, where the Heroes fought,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To feast their eyes with looking on the fray:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Nor did they to their Judgement-Place repair
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ By the ash Igdrasil, in Idaן¿½s plain,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Where they hold council, and give laws for men:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But they went, Odin first, the rest behind,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To the hall Gladheim, which is built of gold;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Where are in circle rangן¿½d twelve golden chairs,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And in the midst one higher, Odinן¿½s throne:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ There all the Gods in silence sate them down;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And thus the Father of the Ages spake:

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Go quickly, Gods, bring wood to the seashore,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With all, which it beseems the dead to have
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And make a funeral pile on Balderן¿½s ship.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ On the twelfth day the Gods shall burn his corpse.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But Hermod, thou, take Sleipner, and ride down
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To Helaן¿½s kingdom, to ask Balder back.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So said he; and the Gods arose, and took
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Axes and ropes, and at their head came Thor,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Shouldering his Hammer, which the Giants know:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Forth wended they, and drove their steeds before:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And up the dewy mountain tracks they farן¿½d
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To the dark forests, in the early dawn;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And up and down and side and slant they roamן¿½d:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And from the glens all day an echo came
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of crashing falls; for with his hammer Thor
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Smote ן¿½mid the rocks the lichen-bearded pines
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And burst their roots; while to their tops the Gods
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Made fast the woven ropes, and halן¿½d them down,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And loppן¿½d their boughs, and clove them on the sward,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And bound the logs behind their steeds to draw,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And drove them homeward; and the snorting steeds
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Went straining through the crackling brushwood down,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And by the darkling forest paths the Gods
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Followן¿½d, and on their shoulders carried boughs.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And they came out upon the plain, and passן¿½d
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Asgard, and led their horses to the beach,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And loosן¿½d them of their loads on the seashore,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And rangן¿½d the wood in stacks by Balderן¿½s ship;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And every God went home to his own house.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But when the Gods were to the forest gone
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Hermod led Sleipner from Valhalla forth
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And saddled him; before that, Sleipner brookן¿½d
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ No meaner hand than Odinן¿½s on his mane,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ On his broad back no lesser rider bore:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Yet docile now he stood at Hermodן¿½s side,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Arching his neck, and glad to be bestrode,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Knowing the God they went to seek, how dear.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But Hermod mounted him, and sadly farן¿½d,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In silence, up the dark untravellן¿½d road
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Which branches from the north of Heaven, and went
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ All day; and Daylight wanן¿½d, and Night came on.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And all that night he rode, and journeyן¿½d so,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Nine days, nine nights, towards the northern ice,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Through valleys deep-engulphן¿½d, by roaring streams:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And on the tenth morn ho beheld the bridge
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Which spans with golden arches Giallן¿½s stream,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And on the bridge a Damsel watching armן¿½d,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In the strait passage, at the further end,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Where the road issues between walling rocks.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Scant space that Warder left for passers by;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But, as when cowherds in October drive
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Their kine across a snowy mountain pass
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To winter pasture on the southern side,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And on the ridge a wagon chokes the way,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Wedgן¿½d in the snow; then painfully the hinds
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With goad and shouting urge their cattle past,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Plunging through deep untrodden banks of snow
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To right and left, and warm steam fills the air
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So on the bridge that Damsel blockן¿½d the way,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And questionן¿½d Hermod as he came, and said:

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Who art thou on thy black and fiery horse
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Under whose hoofs the bridge oן¿½er Giallן¿½s stream
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Rumbles and shakes? Tell me thy race and home.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But yestermorn five troops of dead passן¿½d by
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Bound on their way below to Helaן¿½s realm,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Nor shook the bridge so much as thou alone.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And thou hast flesh and colour on thy cheeks
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Like men who live and draw the vital air;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Nor lookן¿½st thou pale and wan, like men deceasן¿½d,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Souls bound below, my daily passers here.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And the fleet-footed Hermod answerן¿½d her:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½O Damsel, Hermod am I callן¿½d, the son
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of Odin; and my high-roofן¿½d house is built
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Far hence, in Asgard, in the City of Gods:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And Sleipner, Odinן¿½s horse, is this I ride.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And I come, sent this road on Balderן¿½s track:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Say then, if he hath crossן¿½d thy bridge or no?ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He spake; the Warder of the bridge replied:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½O Hermod, rarely do the feet of Gods
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Or of the horses of the Gods resound
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Upon my bridge; and, when they cross, I know.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Balder hath gone this way, and taן¿½en the road
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Below there, to the north, toward Helaן¿½s realm.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ From here the cold white mist can be discernן¿½d,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Not lit with sun, but through the darksome air
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ By the dim vapour-blotted light of stars,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Which hangs over the ice where lies the road.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For in that ice are lost those northern streams
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Freezing and ridging in their onward flow,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Which from the fountain of Vergelmer run,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The spring that bubbles up by Helaן¿½s throne.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ There are the joyless seats, the haunt of ghosts,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Helaן¿½s pale swarms; and there was Balder bound.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Ride on; pass free: but he by this is there.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ She spake, and steppן¿½d aside, and left him room.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And Hermod greeted her, and gallopן¿½d by
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Across the bridge; then she took post again.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But northward Hermod rode, the way below:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And oן¿½er a darksome tract, which knows no sun,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But by the blotted light of stars, he farן¿½d;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And he came down to Oceanן¿½s northern strand
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ At the drear ice, beyond the Giantsן¿½ home:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Thence on he journeyן¿½d oן¿½er the fields of ice
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Still north, until he met a stretching wall
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Barring his way, and in the wall a grate.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Then he dismounted, and drew tight the girths,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ On the smooth ice, of Sleipner, Odinן¿½s horse,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And made him leap the grate, and came within.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And he beheld spread round him Helaן¿½s realm,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The plains of Niflheim, where dwell the dead,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And heard the thunder of the streams of Hell.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For near the wall the river of Roaring flows,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Outmost: the others near the centre run
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The Storm, the Abyss, the Howling, and the Pain:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Those flow by Helaן¿½s throne, and near their spring.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And from the dark flockן¿½d up the shadowy tribes:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And as the swallows crowd the bulrush-beds
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of some clear river, issuing from a lake,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ On autumn days, before they cross the sea;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And to each bulrush-crest a swallow hangs
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Swinging, and others skim the river streams,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And their quick twittering fills the banks and shores
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So around Hermod swarmן¿½d the twittering ghosts.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Women, and infants, and young men who died
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Too soon for fame, with white ungraven shields;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And old men, known to Glory, but their star
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Betrayן¿½d them, and of wasting age they died,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Not wounds: yet, dying, they their armour wore,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And now have chief regard in Helaן¿½s realm.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Behind flockן¿½d wrangling up a piteous crew,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Greeted of none, disfeaturן¿½d and forlorn
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Cowards, who were in sloughs interrן¿½d alive:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And round them still the wattled hurdles hung
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Wherewith they stampן¿½d them down, and trod them deep,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To hide their shameful memory from men.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But all he passן¿½d unhailן¿½d, and reachן¿½d the throne
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of Hela, and saw, near it, Balder crownן¿½d,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And Hela sat thereon, with countenance stern;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And thus bespake him first the solemn Queen:

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Unhappy, how hast thou endurן¿½d to leave
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The light, and journey to the cheerless land
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Where idly flit about the feeble shades
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ How didst thou cross the bridge oן¿½er Giallן¿½s stream,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Being alive, and come to Oceanן¿½s shore?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Or how oן¿½erleap the grate that bars the wall?ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ She spake: but down off Sleipner Hermod sprang,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And fell before her feet, and claspן¿½d her knees;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And spake, and mild entreated her, and said:

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½O Hela, wherefore should the Gods declare
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Their errands to each other, or the ways
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ They go? the errand and the way is known.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Thou knowן¿½st, thou knowן¿½st, what grief we have in Heaven
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For Balder, whom thou holdן¿½st by right below:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Restore him, for what part fulfils he here?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Shall he shed cheer over the cheerless seats,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And touch the apathetic ghosts with joy?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Not for such end, O Queen, thou holdן¿½st thy realm.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For Heaven was Balder born, the City of Gods
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And Heroes, where they live in light and joy:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Thither restore him, for his place is there.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He spoke; and grave replied the solemn Queen:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Hermod, for he thou art, thou Son of Heaven!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A strange unlikely errand, sure, is thine.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Do the Gods send to me to make them blest?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Small bliss my race hath of the Gods obtainן¿½d.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Three mighty children to my Father Lok
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Did Angerbode, the Giantess, bring forth
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Fenris the Wolf, the Serpent huge, and Me:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of these the Serpent in the sea ye cast,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Who since in your despite hath waxן¿½d amain,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And now with gleaming ring enfolds the world:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Me on this cheerless nether world ye threw
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And gave me nine unlighted realms to rule:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ While on his island in the lake, afar,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Made fast to the borן¿½d crag, by wile not strength
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Subduן¿½d, with limber chains lives Fenris bound.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Lok still subsists in Heaven, our Father wise,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Your mate, though loathן¿½d, and feasts in Odinן¿½s hall;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But him too foes await, and netted snares,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And in a cave a bed of needle rocks,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And oן¿½er his visage serpents dropping gall.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Yet he shall one day rise, and burst his bonds,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And with himself set us his offspring free,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ When he guides Muspelן¿½s children to their bourne.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Till then in peril or in pain we live,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Wrought by the Gods: and ask the Gods our aid?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Howbeit we abide our day: till then,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We do not as some feebler haters do,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Seek to afflict our foes with petty pangs,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Helpless to better us, or ruin them.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Come then; if Balder was so dear belovן¿½d,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And this is true, and such a loss is Heavenן¿½s
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Hear, how to Heaven may Balder be restorן¿½d.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Show me through all the world the signs of grief:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Fails but one thing to grieve, here Balder stops:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Let all that lives and moves upon the earth
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Weep him, and all that is without life weep:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Let Gods, men, brutes, beweep him; plants and stones.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So shall I know the lost was dear indeed,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And bend my heart, and give him back to Heaven.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ She spake; and Hermod answerן¿½d her, and said:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Hela, such as thou sayן¿½st, the terms shall be.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But come, declare me this, and truly tell:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ May I, ere I depart, bid Balder hail
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Or is it here withheld to greet the dead?ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He spake; and straightway Hela answerן¿½d him:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Hermod, greet Balder if thou wilt, and hold
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Converse: his speech remains, though he he dead.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And straight to Balder Hermod turnן¿½d, and spake:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Even in the abode of Death, O Balder, hail!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Thou hearן¿½st, if hearing, like as speech, is thine,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The terms of thy releasement hence to Heaven:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Fear nothing but that all shall be fulfillן¿½d.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For not unmindful of thee are the Gods
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Who see the light, and blest in Asgard dwell;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Even here they seek thee out, in Helaן¿½s realm.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And sure of all the happiest far art thou
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Who ever have been known in Earth or Heaven:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Alive, thou wert of Gods the most belovן¿½d:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And now thou sittest crownן¿½d by Helaן¿½s side,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Here, and hast honour among all the dead.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He spake; and Balder utterן¿½d him reply,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But feebly, as a voice far off; he said:

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Hermod the nimble, gild me not my death.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Better to live a slave, a capturן¿½d man,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Who scatters rushes in a masterן¿½s ball,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Than be a crownן¿½d king here, and rule the dead.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And now I count not of these terms as safe
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To be fulfillן¿½d, nor my return as sure,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Though I be lovן¿½d, and many mourn my death:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For double-minded ever was the seed
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of Lok, and double are the gifts they give.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Howbeit, report thy message; and therewith,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To Odin, to my Father, take this ring,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Memorial of me, whether savן¿½d or no:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And tell the Heaven-born Gods how thou hast seen
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Me sitting here below by Hela s side,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Crownן¿½d, having honour among all the dead.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He spake, and raisן¿½d his hand, and gave the ring.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And with inscrutable regard the Queen
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of Hell beheld them, and the ghosts stood dumb.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But Hermod took the ring, and yet once more
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Kneelן¿½d and did homage to the solemn Queen;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Then mounted Sleipner, and set forth to ride
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Back, through the astonishן¿½d tribes of dead, to Heaven.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And to the wall he came, and found the grate
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Lifted, and issued on the fields of ice;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And oן¿½er the ice he farן¿½d to Oceanן¿½s strand,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And up from thence, a wet and misty road,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To the armן¿½d Damselן¿½s bridge, and Giallן¿½s stream.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Worse was that way to go than to return,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For him: for others all return is barrן¿½d.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Nine days he took to go, two to return;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And on the twelfth morn saw the light of Heaven.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And as a traveller in the early dawn
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To the steep edge of some great valley comes
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Through which a river flows, and sees beneath
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Clouds of white rolling vapours fill the vale,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But oן¿½er them, on the farther slope, descries
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Vineyards, and crofts, and pastures, bright with sun
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So Hermod, oן¿½er the fog between, saw Heaven.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And Sleipner snorted, for he smelt the air
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of Heaven: and mightily, as wingן¿½d, he flew.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And Hermod saw the towers of Asgard rise:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And he drew near, and heard no living voice
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In Asgard; and the golden halls were dumb.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Then Hermod knew what labour held the Gods:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And through the empty streets he rode, and passן¿½d
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Under the gate-house to the sands, and found
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The Gods on the seashore by Balderן¿½s ship.



ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ III

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ FUNERAL

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ THE GODS held talk together, groupן¿½d in knots,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Round Balderן¿½s corpse, which they had thither borne;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And Hermod came down towards them from the gate.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And Lok, the Father of the Serpent, first
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Beheld him come, and to his neighbour spake:

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½See, here is Hermod, who comes single back
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ From Hell; and shall I tell thee how he seems
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Like as a farmer, who hath lost his dog,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Some morn, at market, in a crowded town
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Through many streets the poor beast runs in vain,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And follows this man after that, for hours;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And, late at evening, spent and panting, falls
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Before a strangerן¿½s threshold, not his home,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With flanks a-tremble, and his slender tongue
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Hangs quivering out between his dust-smearן¿½d jaws,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And piteously he eyes the passers by:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But home his master comes to his own farm,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Far in the country, wondering where he is
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So Hermod comes to-day unfollowן¿½d home.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And straight his neighbour, movן¿½d with wrath, replied:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Deceiver, fair in form, but false in heart,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Enemy, Mocker, whom, though Gods, we hate
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Peace, lest our Father Odin hear thee gibe.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Would I might see him snatch thee in his hand,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And bind thy carcase, like a bale, with cords,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And hurl thee in a lake, to sink or swim.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ If clear from plotting Balderן¿½s death, to swim;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But deep, if thou devisedst it, to drown,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And perish, against fate, before thy day!ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So they two soft to one another spake.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But Odin lookן¿½d toward the land, and saw
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ His messenger; and he stood forth, and cried:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And Hermod came, and leapt from Sleipner down,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And in his Fatherן¿½s hand put Sleipnerן¿½s rein,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And greeted Odin and the Gods, and said:

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Odin, my Father, and ye, Gods of Heaven!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Lo, home, having performן¿½d your will, I come.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Into the joyless kingdom have I been,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Below, and lookן¿½d upon the shadowy tribes
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of ghosts, and communן¿½d with their solemn Queen;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And to your prayer she sends you this reply:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Show her through all the world the signs of grief:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Fails but one thing to grieve, there Balder stops.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Let Gods, men, brutes, beweep hint, plants and stones.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So shall she know your loss was dear indeed,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And bend her heart, and give you Balder back.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He spoke; and all the Gods to Odin lookן¿½d:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And straight the Father of the Ages said:

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Ye Gods, these terms may keep another day.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But now, put on your arms, and mount your steeds,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And in procession all come near, and weep
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Balder; for that is what the dead desire.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ When ye enough have wept, then build a pile
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of the heapן¿½d wood, and burn his corpse with fire
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Out of our sight; that we may turn from grief,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And lead, as erst, our daily life in Heaven.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He spoke; and the Gods armן¿½d: and Odin donnן¿½d
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ His dazzling corslet and his helm of gold,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And led the way on Sleipner: and the rest
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Followן¿½d, in tears, their Father and their King.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And thrice in arms around the dead they rode,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Weeping; the sands were wetted, and their arms,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With their thick-falling tears: so good a friend
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ They mournן¿½d that day, so bright, so lovן¿½d a God.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And Odin came, and laid his kingly hands
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ On Balderן¿½s breast, and thus began the wail:

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Farewell, O Balder, bright and lovן¿½d, my Son!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In that great day, the Twilight of the Gods.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ When Muspelן¿½s children shall beleaguer Heaven,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Then we shall miss thy counsel and thy arm.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Thou camest near the next, O Warrior Thor!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Shouldering thy Hammer, in thy chariot drawn,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Swaying the long-hairן¿½d Goats with silverן¿½d rein;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And over Balderן¿½s corpse these words didst say:

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Brother, thou dwellest in the darksome land,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And talkest with the feeble tribes of ghosts,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Now, and I know not how they prize thee there,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But here, I know, thou wilt be missן¿½d and mournן¿½d.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For haughty spirits and high wraths are rife
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Among the Gods and Heroes here in Heaven,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As among those, whose joy and work is war:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And daily strifes arise, and angry words:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But from thy lips, O Balder, night or day,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Heard no one ever an injurious word
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To God or Hero, but thou keptest back
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The others, labouring to compose their brawls.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Be ye then kind, as Balder too was kind:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For we lose him, who smoothן¿½d all strife in Heaven.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He spake: and all the Gods assenting wailן¿½d.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And Freya next came nigh, with golden tears:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The loveliest Goddess she in Heaven, by all
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Most honourן¿½d after Frea, Odinן¿½s wife:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Her long ago the wandering Oder took
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To mate, but left her to roam distant lands;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Since then she seeks him, and weeps tears of gold:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Names hath she many; Vanadis on earth
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ They call her; Freya is her name in Heaven:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ She in her hands took Balderן¿½s head, and spake:

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Balder, my brother, thou art gone a road
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Unknown and long, and haply on that way
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ My long-lost wandering Oder thou hast met,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For in the paths of Heaven he is not found.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Oh, if it be so, tell him what thou wert
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To his neglected wife, and what he is,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And wring his heart with shame, to hear thy word.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For he, my husband, left me here to pine,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Not long a wife, when his unquiet heart
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ First drove him from me into distant lands.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Since then I vainly seek him through the world,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And weep from shore to shore my golden tears,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But neither god nor mortal heeds my pain.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Thou only, Balder, wert for ever kind,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To take my hand, and wipe my tears, and say:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Weep not, O Freya, weep no golden tears!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ One day the wandering Oder will return,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Or thou wilt find him in thy faithful search
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ On some great road, or resting in an inn,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Or at a ford, or sleeping by a tree.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So Balder said; but Oder, well I know,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ My truant Oder I shall see no more
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To the worldן¿½s end; and Balder now is gone;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And I am left uncomforted in Heaven.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ She spake; and all the Goddesses bewailן¿½d.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Last, from among the Heroes one came near,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ No God, but of the Hero-troop the chief
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Regner, who swept the northern sea with fleets,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And rulן¿½d oן¿½er Denmark and the heathy isles,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Living; but Ella capturן¿½d him and slew:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A king, whose fame then fillן¿½d the vast of Heaven,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Now time obscures it, and menן¿½s later deeds:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He last approachן¿½d the corpse, and spake, and said:

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Balder, there yet are many Scalds in Heaven
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Still left, and that chief Scald, thy brother Brage,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Whom we may bid to sing, though thou art gone:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And all these gladly, while we drink, we hear,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ After the feast is done, in Odinן¿½s hall:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But they harp ever on one string, and wake
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Remembrance in our soul of wars alone,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Such as on earth we valiantly have wagן¿½d,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And blood, and ringing blows, and violent death:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But when thou sangest, Balder, thou didst strike
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Another note, and, like a bird in spring,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Thy voice of joyance minded us, and youth,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And wife, and children, and our ancient home.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Yes, and I too rememberן¿½d then no more
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ My dungeon, where the serpents stung me dead,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Nor Ellaן¿½s victory on the English coast;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But I heard Thora laugh in Gothland Isle;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And saw my shepherdess, Aslauga, tend
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Her flock along the white Norwegian beach:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Tears started to mine eyes with yearning joy
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Therefore with grateful heart I mourn thee dead.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So Regner spake, and all the Heroes groanן¿½d.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But now the sun had passן¿½d the height of Heaven,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And soon had all that day been spent in wail;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But then the Father of the Ages said:

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Ye Gods, there well may be too much of wail.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Bring now the gatherן¿½d wood to Balderן¿½s ship;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Heap on the deck the logs, and build the pyre.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But when the Gods and Heroes heard, they brought
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The wood to Balderן¿½s ship, and built a pile,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Full the deckן¿½s breadth, and lofty; then the corpse
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of Balder on the highest top they laid,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With Nanna on his right, and on his left
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Hoder, his brother, whom his own hand slew.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And they set jars of wine and oil to lean
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Against the bodies, and stuck torches near,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Splinters of pine-wood, soakן¿½d with turpentine;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And brought his arms and gold, and all his stuff,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And slew the dogs which at his table fed,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And his horse, Balderן¿½s horse, whom most he lovן¿½d,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And threw them on the pyre, and Odin threw
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A last choice gift thereon, his golden ring.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ They fixt the mast, and hoisted up the sails,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Then they put fire to the wood; and Thor
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Set his stout shoulder hard against the stern
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To push the ship through the thick sand: sparks flew
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ From the deep trench she ploughן¿½d so strong a God
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Furrowן¿½d it and the water gurgled in.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And the Ship floated on the waves, and rockן¿½d:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But in the hills a strong East-Wind arose,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And came down moaning to the sea; first squalls
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Ran black oן¿½er the seaן¿½s face, then steady rushן¿½d
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The breeze, and fillן¿½d the sails, and blew the fire.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And, wreathן¿½d in smoke, the Ship stood out to sea.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Soon with a roaring rose the mighty fire,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And the pile crackled; and between the logs
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Sharp quivering tongues of flame shot out, and leapt,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Curling and darting, higher, until they lickן¿½d
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The summit of the pile, the dead, the mast,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And ate the shrivelling sails; but still the Ship
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Drove on, ablaze, above her hull, with fire.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And the Gods stood upon the beach, and gazן¿½d:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And, while they gazן¿½d, the Sun went lurid down
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Into the smoke-wrapt sea, and Night came on.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Then the wind fell, with night, and there was calm.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But through the dark they watchן¿½d the burning Ship
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Still carried oן¿½er the distant waters on
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Farther and farther, like an Eye of Fire.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And as in the dark night a travelling man
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Who bivouacs in a forest ן¿½mid the hills,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Sees suddenly a spire of flame shoot up
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Out of the black waste forest, far below,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Which woodcutters have lighted near their lodge
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Against the wolves; and all night long it flares:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So flarן¿½d, in the far darkness, Balderן¿½s pyre.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But fainter, as the stars rose high, it burnן¿½d;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The bodies were consumן¿½d, ash chokן¿½d the pile
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And as in a decaying winter fire
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A charrן¿½d log, falling, makes a shower of sparks
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So, with a shower of sparks, the pile fell in,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Reddening the sea around; and all was dark.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But the Gods went by starlight up the shore
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To Asgard, and sate down in Odinן¿½s hall
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ At table, and the funeral-feast began.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ All night they ate the boar Serimnerן¿½s flesh,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And from their horns, with silver rimmן¿½d, drank mead,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Silent, and waited for the sacred Morn.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And Morning over all the world was spread.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Then from their loathן¿½d feast the Gods arose,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And took their horses, and set forth to ride
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Oן¿½er the bridge Bifrost, where is Heimdallן¿½s watch,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To the ash Igdrasil, and Idaן¿½s plain:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Thor came on foot; the rest on horseback rode.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And they found Mimir sitting by his Fount
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of Wisdom, which beneath the ashtree springs;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And saw the Nornies watering the roots
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of that world-shadowing tree with Honey-dew:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ There came the Gods, and sate them down on stones:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And thus the Father of the Ages said:

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Ye Gods, the terms ye know, which Hermod brought.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Accept them or reject them; both have grounds.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Accept them, and they bind us, unfulfillן¿½d,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To leave for ever Balder in the grave,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ An unrecoverן¿½d prisoner, shade with shades.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But how, ye say, should the fulfilment fail?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Smooth sound the terms, and light to be fulfillן¿½d;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For dear-belovן¿½d was Balder while he livן¿½d
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In Heaven and Earth, and who would grudge him tears?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But from the traitorous seed of Lok they come,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ These terms, and I suspect some hidden fraud.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Bethink ye, Gods, is there no other way?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Speak, were not this a way, the way for Gods?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ If I, if Odin, clad in radiant arms,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Mounted on Sleipner, with the Warrior Thor
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Drawn in his car beside me, and my sons,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ All the strong brood of Heaven, to swell my train,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Should make irruption into Helaן¿½s realm,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And set the fields of gloom ablaze with light,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And bring in triumph Balder back to Heaven?ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He spake; and his fierce sons applauded loud.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But Frea, Mother of the Gods, arose,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Daughter and wife of Odin; thus she said:

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Odin, thou Whirlwind, what a threat is this!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Thou threatenest what transcends thy might, even thine.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For of all powers the mightiest far art thou,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Lord over men on Earth, and Gods in Heaven;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Yet even from thee thyself hath been withheld
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ One thing; to undo what thou thyself hast rulן¿½d.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For all which hath been fixt, was fixt by thee:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In the beginning, ere the Gods were born,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Before the Heavens were builded, thou didst slay
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The Giant Ymir, whom the Abyss brought forth,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Thou and thy brethren fierce, the Sons of Bor,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And threw his trunk to choke the abysmal void:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But of his flesh and members thou didst build
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The Earth and Ocean, and above them Heaven:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And from the flaming world, where Muspel reigns,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Thou sentן¿½st and fetchedן¿½st fire, and madest lights,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Sun Moon and Stars, which thou hast hung in Heaven,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Dividing clear the paths of night and day:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And Asgard thou didst build, and Midgard Fort:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Then me thou madן¿½st; of us the Gods were born:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Then, walking by the sea, thou foundest spars
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of wood, and framedן¿½st men, who till the earth,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Or on the sea, the field of pirates, sail:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And all the race of Ymir thou didst drown,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Save one, Bergelmer; he on shipboard fled
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Thy deluge, and from him the Giants sprang;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But all that brood thou hast removן¿½d far off,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And set by Oceanן¿½s utmost marge to dwell:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But Hela into Niflheim thou threwן¿½st,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And gavן¿½st her nine unlighted worlds to rule,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A Queen, and empire over all the dead.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That empire wilt thou now invade, light up
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Her darkness, from her grasp a subject tear?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Try it; but I, for one, will not applaud.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Nor do I merit, Odin, thou shouldן¿½st slight
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Me and my words, though thou be first in Heaven
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For I too am a Goddess, born of thee,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Thine eldest, and of me the Gods are sprung;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And all that is to come I know, but lock
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In my own breast, and have to none revealן¿½d.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Come then; since Hela holds by right her prey,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But offers terms for his release to heaven,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Accept the chance; thou canst no more obtain.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Send through the world thy messengers: entreat
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ All living and unliving things to weep
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For Balder; if thou haply thus mayן¿½st melt
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Hela, and win the lovן¿½d one back to Heaven.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ She spake, and on her face let fall her veil,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And bowן¿½d her head, and sate with folded hands.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Nor did the all-ruling Odin slight her word;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Straightway he spake, and thus addressן¿½d the Gods:

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Go quickly forth through all the world, and pray
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ All living and unliving things to weep
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Balder, if haply he may thus be won.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ When the Gods heard, they straight arose, and took
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Their horses, and rode forth through all the world.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ North south east west they struck, and roamן¿½d the world,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Entreating all things to weep Balderן¿½s death:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And all that livן¿½d, and all without life, wept.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And as in winter, when the frost breaks up,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ At winterן¿½s end, before the spring begins,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And a warm west wind blows, and thaw sets in
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ After an hour a dripping sound is heard
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In all the forests, and the soft-strewn snow
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Under the trees is dibbled thick with holes.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And from the boughs the snowloads shuffle down;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And in fields sloping to the south dark plots
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of grass peep out amid surrounding snow,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And widen, and the peasantן¿½s heart is glad
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So through the world was heard a dripping noise
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of all things weeping to bring Balder back:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And there fell joy upon the Gods to hear.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But Hermod rode with Niord, whom he took
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To show him spits and beaches of the sea
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Far off, where some unwarnן¿½d might fail to weep
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Niord, the God of storms, whom fishers know
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Not born in Heaven; he was in Vanheim rearן¿½d,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With men, but lives a hostage with the Gods:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He knows each frith, and every rocky creek
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Fringן¿½d with dark pines, and sands where seafowl scream:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ They two scourן¿½d every coast, and all things wept.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And they rode home together, through the wood
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of Jarnvid, which to east of Midgard lies
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Bordering the Giants, where the trees are iron;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ There in the wood before a cave they came
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Where sate, in the caveן¿½s mouth, a skinny Hag,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Toothless and old; she gibes the passers by:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Thok is she callן¿½d; but now Lok wore her shape:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ She greeted them the first, and laughן¿½d, and said:

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Ye Gods, good lack, is it so dull in Heaven.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That ye come pleasuring to Thokן¿½s Iron Wood?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Lovers of change ye are, fastidious sprites.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Look, as in some boorן¿½s yard a sweet-breathן¿½d cow
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Whose manger is stuffן¿½d full of good fresh hay
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Snuffs at it daintily, and stoops her head
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To chew the straw, her litter, at her feet
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So ye grow squeamish, Gods, and sniff at Heaven.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ She spake; but Hermod answerן¿½d her and said:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Thok, not for gibes we come, we come for tears.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Balder is dead, and Hela holds her prey,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But will restore, if all things give him tears.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Begrudge not thine; to all was Balder dear.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But, with a louder laugh, the Hag replied:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Is Balder dead? and do ye come for tears?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Thok with dry eyes will weep oן¿½er Balderן¿½s pyre.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Weep him all other things, if weep they will
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I weep him not: let Hela keep her prey!ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ She spake; and to the cavernן¿½s depth she fled,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Mocking: and Hermod knew their toil was vain.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And as seafaring men, who long have wrought
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In the great deep for gain, at last come home,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And towards evening see the headlands rise
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of their own country, and can clear descry
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A fire of witherן¿½d furze which boys have lit
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Upon the cliffs, or smoke of burning weeds
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Out of a tillן¿½d field inland; then the wind
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Catches them, and drives out again to sea:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And they go long days tossing up and down
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Over the grey sea ridges; and the glimpse
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of port they had makes bitterer far their toil
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So the Godsן¿½ cross was bitterer for their joy.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Then, sad at heart, to Niord Hermod spake:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½It is the Accuser Lok, who flouts us all.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Ride back, and tell in Heaven this heavy news.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I must again below, to Helaן¿½s realm.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He spoke; and Niord set forth back to Heaven.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But northward Hermod rode, the way below;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Tho way he knew: and traversן¿½d Giallן¿½s stream,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And down to Ocean gropן¿½d, and crossן¿½d the ice,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And came beneath the wall, and found the grate
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Still lifted; well was his return foreknown.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And once more Hermod saw around him spread
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The joyless plains, and heard the streams of Hell.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But as he enterן¿½d, on the extremest hound
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of Niflheim, he saw one Ghost come near,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Hovering, and stopping oft, as if afraid;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Hoder, the unhappy, whom his own hand slew:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And Hermod lookן¿½d, and knew his brotherן¿½s ghost,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And callן¿½d him by his name, and sternly said:

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Hoder, ill-fated, blind in heart and eyes!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Why tarriest thou to plunge thee in the gulph
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of the deep inner gloom, but flittest here,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In twilight, on the lonely verge of Hell,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Far from the other ghosts, and Helaן¿½s throne?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Doubtless thou fearest to meet Balderן¿½s voice,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Thy brother, whom through folly thou didst slay.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He spoke; but Hoder answerן¿½d him, and said:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Hermod the nimble, dost thou still pursue
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The unhappy with reproach, even in the grave?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For this I died, and fled beneath the gloom,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Not daily to endure abhorring Gods,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Nor with a hateful presence cumber Heaven
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And canst thou not, even here, pass pitying by?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ No less than Balder have I lost the light
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of Heaven, and communion with my kin:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I too had once a wife, and once a child,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And substance, and a golden house in Heaven:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But all I left of my own act, and fled
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Below, and dost thou hate me even here?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Balder upbraids me not, nor hates at all,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Though he has cause, have any cause; but he,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ When that with downcast looks I hither came,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Stretchן¿½d forth his hand, and, with benignant voice,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Welcome, he said, if there be welcome here,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Brother and fellow-sport of Lok with me.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And not to offend thee, Hermod, nor to force
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ My hated converse on thee, came I up
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ From the deep gloom, where I will now return;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But earnestly I longן¿½d to hover near,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Not too far off, when that thou camest by,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To feel the presence of a brother God,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And hear the passage of a horse of Heaven,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For the last time: for here thou comן¿½st no more.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He spake, and turnן¿½d to go to the inner gloom.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But Hermod stayן¿½d him with mild words, and said:

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Thou doest well to chide me, Hoder blind.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Truly thou sayן¿½st, the planning guilty mind
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Was Lokן¿½s; the unwitting hand alone was thine.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But Gods are like the sons of men in this
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ When they have woe, they blame the nearest cause.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Howbeit stay, and be appeasן¿½d; and tell
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Sits Balder still in pomp by Helaן¿½s side,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Or is he mingled with the unnumberן¿½d dead?ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And the blind Hoder answerן¿½d him and spake:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½His place of state remains by Helaן¿½s side,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But empty: for his wife, for Nanna came
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Lately below, and joinן¿½d him; and the Pair
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Frequent the still recesses of the realm
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of Hela, and hold converse undisturbן¿½d.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But they too doubtless, will have breathן¿½d the balm
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Which floats before a visitant from Heaven,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And have drawn upwards to this verge of Hell.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He spake; and, as he ceasן¿½d, a puff of wind
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Rollן¿½d heavily the leaden mist aside
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Round where they stood, and they beheld Two Forms
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Make towards them oן¿½er the stretching cloudy plain.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And Hermod straight perceivן¿½d them, who they were,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Balder and Nanna; and to Balder said:

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Balder, too truly thou foresawן¿½st a snare.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Lok triumphs still, and Hela keeps her prey.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ No more to Asgard shalt thou come, nor lodge
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In thy own house, Breidablik, nor enjoy
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The love all bear towards thee, nor train up
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Forset, thy son, to be belovן¿½d like thee.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Here must thou lie, and wait an endless age.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Therefore for the last time, O Balder, hail!ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He spake; and Balder answerן¿½d him and said:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Hail and farewell, for here thou comן¿½st no more.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Yet mourn not for me, Hermod, when thou sittן¿½st
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In Heaven, nor let the other Gods lament,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As wholly to be pitied, quite forlorn:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For Nanna hath rejoinן¿½d me, who, of old,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In Heaven, was seldom parted from my side;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And still the acceptance follows me, which crowned
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ My former life, and cheers me even here.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The iron frown of Hela is relaxן¿½d
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ When I draw nigh, and the wan tribes of dead
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Trust me, and gladly bring for my award
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Their ineffectual feuds and feeble hates,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Shadows of hates, but they distress them still.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And the fleet-footed Hermod made reply:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Thou hast then all the solace death allows,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Esteem and function: and so far is well.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Yet here thou liest, Balder, underground,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Rusting for ever: and the years roll on,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The generations pass, the ages grow,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And bring us nearer to the final day
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ When from the south shall march the Fiery Band
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And cross the Bridge of Heaven, with Lok for guide,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And Fenris at his heel with broken chain:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ While from the east the Giant Rymer steers
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ His ship, and the great Serpent makes to land;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And all are marshallן¿½d in one flaming square
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Against the Gods, upon the plains of Heaven.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I mourn thee, that thou canst not help us then.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He spake; but Balder answerן¿½d him and said:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Mourn not for me: Mourn, Hermod, for the Gods:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Mourn for the men on Earth, the Gods in Heaven,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Who live, and with their eyes shall see that day.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The day will come, when Asgardן¿½s towers shall fall,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And Odin, and his Sons, the seed of Heaven:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But what were I, to save them in that hour?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ If strength could save them, could not Odin save,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ My Father, and his pride, the Warrior Thor,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Vidar the Silent, the Impetuous Tyr?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I, what were I, when these can naught avail?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Yet, doubtless. when the day of battle comes,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And the two Hosts are marshallן¿½d, and in Heaven
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The golden-crested Cock shall sound alarm,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And his black Brother, Bird from hence reply,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And bucklers clash, and spears begin to pour
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Longing will stir within my breast, though vain.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But not to me so grievous, as, I know,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To other Gods it were, is my enforcן¿½d
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Absence from fields where I could nothing aid:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For I am long since weary of your storm
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of carnage, and find, Hermod, in your life
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Something too much of war and broils, which make
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Life one perpetual fight, a bath of blood.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Mine eyes are dizzy with the arrowy hail;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Mine ears are stunnן¿½d with blows, and sick for calm.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Inactive therefore let me lie, in gloom,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Unarmן¿½d, inglorious: I attend the course
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of ages, and my late return to light,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In times less alien to a spirit mild,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In new-recoverן¿½d seats, the happier day.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He spake; and the fleet Hermod thus replied:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Brother, what seats are these, what happier day?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Tell me, that I may ponder it when gone.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And the ray-crowned Balder answerן¿½d him:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Far to the south, beyond The Blue, there spreads
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Another Heaven, The Boundless: no one yet
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Hath reachן¿½d it: there hereafter shall arise
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The second Asgard, with another name.
